cityCenter.name: Central London
cityCenter.description: Heart of London around Trafalgar Square and Covent Garden

routes.name: Elizabeth line

price.note: Contactless pay-as-you-go; off-peak vs peak. Fares change each March—check tfl.gov.uk

routes.destination: Paddington / Bond Street / Farringdon / Liverpool Street
routes.tips: The sweet spot for most travellers: half the price of Heathrow Express, air-conditioned, and it runs straight through the West End and City—no transfer at Paddington needed. Just tap in with a contactless card.
pros: Modern, air-conditioned trains with luggage space
cons: Slower than Heathrow Express to Paddington

routes.name: Heathrow Express

price.note: Walk-up single ~£25; advance online fares from ~£10 if booked weeks ahead

routes.tips: Fastest rail link—15 minutes non-stop from T2/T3 to Paddington—but you'll likely need the Tube onward from there. Book online in advance for big savings; walk-up fares are steep.
pros: Cheap if booked well in advance
cons: Only serves Paddington—most trips need an onward Tube hop

routes.name: Piccadilly line (Underground)

price.note: Contactless pay-as-you-go, off-peak vs peak; counts toward the daily cap

routes.destination: Zone 1 (Piccadilly Circus, Covent Garden, King's Cross)
routes.tips: The budget classic. Slow but dirt cheap, and it drops you right in the West End without changing. Avoid weekday rush hours with big bags—the trains are ordinary Tube stock with no luggage racks.
pros: Counts toward your contactless daily cap
cons: Crowded at rush hour, minimal luggage space

routes.name: Black cab
routes.tips: Use the official ranks outside arrivals. Iconic, spacious, and drivers know every street thanks to 'the Knowledge'—but the M4 into town can crawl, and the meter keeps running.
cons: Traffic can double the journey time

routes.name: Uber/Bolt
routes.tips: Meet at the designated rideshare pickup zones (follow airport signage—they move occasionally). Usually noticeably cheaper than a black cab, but surge pricing after late-night arrivals can erase the gap.
pros: Cheaper than black cabs most of the time

routes.name: Thameslink
routes.destination: London Bridge / Blackfriars / Farringdon / St Pancras
routes.tips: The smart pick: cheaper than Gatwick Express, barely slower, and it stops at more useful stations—London Bridge for the South Bank, Farringdon to connect with the Elizabeth line, St Pancras for Eurostar connections. Tap in with contactless.
pros: Serves London Bridge, Blackfriars, Farringdon and St Pancras

routes.name: Gatwick Express

price.note: ~£21 single online; slightly more at the machine. ~10% off booking online

routes.tips: Non-stop to Victoria in about half an hour. Worth it if your hotel is near Victoria; otherwise Thameslink covers more of the city for roughly half the price and only a few minutes more.

routes.name: Taxi / rideshare

price.note: Pre-booked minicabs cheaper than metered black cabs for this distance

routes.tips: It's a long, expensive schlep up the M23—rarely worth it unless you land after the trains stop or you're a group of four with mountains of luggage. Pre-book a fixed-price minicab rather than hailing.

routes.name: Stansted Express

price.note: Advance online singles from ~£13; walk-up fares can approach double—book ahead

routes.tips: The only fast way in from Stansted. Book online before you fly—the price gap between advance and walk-up is brutal. Change at Tottenham Hale for the Victoria line if you're headed to the West End.
pros: Tottenham Hale interchange for the Victoria line
cons: Liverpool Street is east—most visitors need an onward Tube

routes.name: Airport coaches (National Express / Airport Bus Express)
routes.tips: The budget-airline classic: cheap, runs all night, and matches the schedules of early Ryanair departures. Allow generous buffer time—the M11 and East London traffic are unpredictable.
pros: Direct to several parts of London
cons: Can be full at peak times—book a seat

routes.name: Taxi / rideshare

price.note: Pre-book a fixed price; metered/rideshare varies with demand

routes.tips: Only sensible for groups or middle-of-the-night arrivals outside coach hours. Stansted is the furthest major airport from the centre—the fare reflects it.

routes.name: DLR (Docklands Light Railway)

price.note: Contactless pay-as-you-go (Zone 3 to Zone 1), off-peak vs peak

routes.destination: Bank (change at Canning Town for the Jubilee line)
routes.tips: The station is literally attached to the terminal—you can be on a driverless train five minutes after clearing arrivals. Ride up front for skyline views over Docklands. Tap in with contactless.
pros: Cheap regular TfL fare, counts toward the daily cap
Quick connections at Canning Town and Bank
cons: Usually one change for the West End
Small trains, limited luggage space at rush hour

routes.name: Black cab / rideshare

price.note: Metered black cab; rideshare usually a bit less

routes.tips: The closest airport to the centre, so this is the one place a cab is a defensible splurge—especially to the City or Canary Wharf, which are minutes away.
pros: Shortest airport taxi ride in London
cons: Still several times the DLR fare
East London traffic at rush hour

routes.name: Underground (King's Cross St Pancras)

price.note: Contactless Zone 1 single; counts toward the daily cap

routes.destination: Anywhere in central London (6 Tube lines)
routes.tips: Eurostar from Paris (~2h15), Brussels or Amsterdam lands you already in Zone 1. King's Cross St Pancras is served by six Tube lines—Victoria, Piccadilly, Northern, Circle, Metropolitan, Hammersmith & City—so almost everywhere is one train away. Just walk across the concourse and tap in.
pros: You arrive already in central London
Six Tube lines under one roof
No border queues on arrival—passport control happens before departure
cons: Station is huge—allow a few minutes to navigate

routes.name: Black cab

price.note: Metered; most central hotels within this range

routes.tips: The taxi rank is at street level on Midland Road. With luggage after a long train ride, a short metered hop to your hotel is an affordable treat here—unlike from the airports.
pros: No stairs or escalators with luggage
cons: Central traffic can make the Tube faster
Queue at the rank after big arrivals

generalTips: Skip paper tickets entirely: tap a contactless card or phone on the yellow readers for the Tube, buses, DLR, Elizabeth line and most trains. Fares are capped automatically—around £8.90/day for Zones 1-2 as of early 2026 (TfL fares typically change each March, so check tfl.gov.uk). Always use the same card/device for every tap.
The Tube map is organised in zones radiating from Zone 1 (the centre). Almost everything visitors want is in Zones 1-2; Heathrow is out in Zone 6. Off-peak fares apply outside weekday rush hours.
Buses are the bargain: a flat ~£1.75 per ride with unlimited changes within an hour thanks to the Hopper fare, and the top deck of a double-decker beats any tour bus.

Black cabs can be hailed on the street when the yellow 'TAXI' light is on—drivers pass the legendary 'Knowledge' test and take card payments. Uber and Bolt are usually cheaper but can't use bus lanes; for short central hops a black cab often wins on time.
Santander Cycles (the red 'Boris bikes') cost about £1.65 per 30-minute ride or £3.50 for a day of unlimited short rides—brilliant for the parks. Remember: ride on the left.

Walking is genuinely underrated: the Tube map distorts distances, and central stations like Covent Garden and Leicester Square are a 4-minute stroll apart. When in doubt above ground, walk it—London reveals itself between stations.
Avoid the Tube with luggage at weekday rush hours (7:30-9:30am, 5-7pm), stand on the right on escalators, and mind the gap.
